text:
We investigated the effects of intestinal microbiota on the cerebral metabolome using GF and colonized (Ex-GF) mice. Metabolic profiling of the brain from these mice was obtained by using CE-TOFMS and identified 196 LMW metabolites in the brain of these mice, and among them, 38 were found to have altered levels in colonized mice as compared with their GF counterparts.

Previously we determined that GF mice had twice as much dopamine and a unique pattern of pipecolic acid and tryptophan in their brains, cerebrospinal fluid and plasma as compared with Ex-GF mice. Here we report that having a gut microbiome changes the pattern of cerebral energy metabolism: compared with GF mice, Ex-GF mice had significantly lower levels of glycolytic intermediates and less ATP and NADH, pointing to an increase in the metabolic flux of the glycolytic pathway and an increased energy demand in their brains. Brain-synthesized versus blood-derived metabolic changes? Even though some metabolites were produced exclusively in the brain, other intermediates having the same ratios in different organs of the gut, plasma, and brain indicated that some blood-derived components may be missing the BBB hurdle. Overall, our studies supported the role of the microbiota-gut-brain axis in maintaining a proper pattern of cerebral energy metabolism, an apparatus important for normal development and function of the brain, and thus its dysregulation important in various neuro-psychiatric disorders.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
